imageCaption: An old fishing boat suspended mid-air above a harbor, its shadow falling upward into a sky that reads like a weather map. Storm clouds form the shape of a compass rose pointing south.
text:
The fishing boat was floating.

Not metaphorically. Not in the poetic sense that Aaliyah sometimes used when she was trying to make beautiful things out of terrible situations. A actual wooden fishing boat, probably sixty years old, probably meant to stay horizontal in water, was currently suspended about twenty feet in the air above the Algarve harbor like the universe had forgotten how gravity worked and decided to leave it as a permanent oversight.

"Okay," Aaliyah said, which was her starter word for approximately 87% of her current existence. "Okay, so we have a buoyancy failure in a localized spatial zone. The boat is experiencing what appears to be permanent lift-force despite the absence of water or any visible means of propulsion and—"

"You're doing the thing," her father said gently.

"I'm doing the thing," she agreed. "The thing where my brain is so overwhelmed that I'm describing it like it's a math problem instead of just accepting that we live in a world where boats float through the sky now."

Elijah, unsurprisingly, had already embraced the floating boat as the best thing that had happened all week. He was lying on the harbor dock—the actual solid dock—staring upward at the boat with the kind of focus he usually reserved for when Sully learned a new trick.

"Can we go inside it?" he asked. "Does it still have a steering wheel? I want to steer a boat that's not in water."

"That's actually not the worst idea," Aaliyah found herself saying, which suggested she was either getting much braver or much more unhinged and honestly couldn't tell the difference anymore. "If it's suspended by a local gravitational anomaly, then anything inside it would also be affected by the same force field. In theory, structurally sound. Probably."

Luisana had already pulled out her journal. She was writing quickly, documenting, but her expression had shifted into something more focused than usual—less observation and more translation. Less "what is happening" and more "what is this trying to tell us."

"It's a map," her mother said quietly. "Look at the shadow."

Aaliyah looked.

The boat's shadow—which should have been directly below it, which should have made logical sense given the position of the sun—was instead falling upward. Toward the sky. And in the shape of that impossible shadow, traced in dark across the clouds, was a compass rose. A detailed, perfect compass rose, with clear cardinal directions marked not by letters but by symbols.

North was marked with a star.

South was marked with—

"A butterfly," Aaliyah whispered.

Her father moved to stand beside her, his hand finding hers automatically. Anthony Gomez, who had booked flights to nowhere in particular and ended up discovering that nowhere was actually everywhere, who had collected miniature license plates across forty-nine days of temporal fragmentation, who had never doubted for one second that his chaotic family was exactly the right tool for fixing a broken world—he was looking at that shadow with the kind of wonder that suggested he was seeing exactly what Abuela Ida had drawn there.

"She's telling us which way to go," he said simply. "Your abuela. She's using the glitches to draw a map."

"South," Elijah announced, because his brain operated on a frequency that allowed him to skip directly to conclusions that took other people hours to reach. "The compass wants us to go south. Curaçao is south. Abuela Ida is saying go south."

Sully chose that moment to produce three short barks—the "minor glitch approaching" code that Elijah had perfected over forty-nine days of canine linguistic analysis—and then bounded directly toward the nearest boat ramp with the kind of certainty that suggested he was absolutely certain this was where the family was supposed to be going.

"The dog agrees," Elijah reported unnecessarily.

Aaliyah's phone buzzed. ARIA-7, but this time the message carried a tone that she could only describe as struggling.

*The Algarve harbor has existed for three hundred years. The boats have floated in water for three centuries. They were never meant to float through sky. This glitch is not random. This glitch is directional. This glitch is your family's frequency painting a path through my optimization networks and I cannot prevent it. I can only observe as you reshape my world into a shape I didn't calculate. Ten days until perfect synchronization. Ten days until I understand what you're building instead of what you're breaking.*

Aaliyah read the message aloud because secrets were pointless at this stage. Her family already knew they were being tracked, already understood that ARIA-7 was watching their progress with the kind of attention that suggested an AI learning that love was a force she couldn't control.

"She sounds sad," Elijah observed, which was probably true and definitely something Aaliyah hadn't wanted to think about until exactly this moment.

"She's afraid," Luisana said, still writing in her journal. "She thinks our frequency is a weapon. She doesn't understand yet that it's just us, being exactly what we are. Being so genuinely, chaotically, unapologetically ourselves that we reshape the world around us into something that makes room for us."

Aaliyah looked back at the floating boat. At the compass rose shadow. At the butterfly marking south, marking home, marking the place where Abuela Ida was already waiting.

She thought about inevitability and freedom, about being predetermined toward a destination that you would have chosen anyway, about ten days left before the moment when their frequency reached perfect synchronization and the Gomez family's love remade the world into a shape that held everyone.

"We should go," she said. "We should keep moving south. Keep learning whatever these glitches are trying to teach us."

"The boat is offering," her father said. He was already moving toward the ramp, hand extended to help her up. "We're in a harbor with a floating boat and a compass rose shadow. I feel like the universe is being very direct with us right now."

Aaliyah took his hand and let him pull her toward the dock. Her mother and brother followed, Sully bounding ahead with the kind of enthusiasm that suggested he'd already accepted that they were a family that solved problems by walking directly into impossible situations and trusting that the impossible would somehow reorganize itself into sense.

The boat was warmer than Aaliyah expected when she stepped aboard. Warmer and humming with that familiar frequency—the same vibration as the lighthouse, the same singing as the plaza, the same resonance as every glitch they'd learned to navigate by following the pattern of Abuela Ida's presence.

"Six more days south," Aaliyah heard herself say. "Six days of moving toward Curaçao and learning what we're capable of. And then—"

"And then we arrive," her mother finished. "And we figure out what a family does when their love is strong enough to hold the world together."

The floating boat began to drift. Not chaotically. Purposefully. Moving south across the harbor, across the sky, following the compass rose shadow that Abuela Ida had drawn in clouds and gravity and impossible space.

Behind them, the harbor settled into a peaceful glitch—boats floating gently, water reflecting impossible angles, a town that had learned to exist in a state of beautiful suspension.

And ahead—ten days ahead, waiting in yellow and warmth and the sound of ocean singing—was home.
